meta.topic: Wh-questions with who, whom, which, how and whose

step_1_context.text: In wh-questions, who is usually the subject and whom is usually the object. Whom is more formal than who:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule: In wh-questions, who is usually the subject and whom is usually the object.
step_2_explanation.formula:
Which is used to refer to people or things when we want to identify one of a group:
 • 'Which is your brother?' 'The one next to Luka.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake:
When we use who or what as subjects, the verb that follows is usually singular, even if a plural answer is expected:
 • What is there to see in the town? (expects an answer giving a number of things to see)
 However, the verb can be plural in echo questions (see Unit 27E) or when who and what functi

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 26.1.1 To ___ should the documents be sent?
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Formal context with preposition before the wh-word.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 26.1.2 ___ of you is Dr Hansen? I have a message for you.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Identifying a person from a group.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 26.1.4 A: Is your sister at home? B: What / ___ one do you want to speak to?
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Which one is used when choosing between a limited set.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 26.1.6 ___ will captain the team if Zeinab isn't available?
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Subject of the sentence asking about a person.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 26.2.1 What ___ those cakes made from? (be)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Who/What as subject usually takes a singular verb.
